texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolites are porous crystalline aluminosilicates of natural or synthetic origin with a three-dimensional framework of silicon-aluminum-oxygen tetrahedra. In addition to their superior thermal stability, the zeolite framework is built of pores and channels in the molecular dimension range. A negative charge results due to substitution of Al 3+ for Si 4+ in the framework. Nonframework cations are required for charge compensation and the cations are exchangeable. These properties make zeolites interesting for catalysis and separation applications [1-4].
texts.text: Zeolites are porous crystalline aluminosilicates of natural or synthetic origin with a three-dimensional framework of silicon-aluminum-oxygen tetrahedra. In addition to their superior thermal stability, the zeolite framework is built of pores and channels in the molecular dimension range. A negative charge results due to substitution of Al 3+ for Si 4+ in the framework. Nonframework cations are required for charge compensation and the cations are exchangeable. These properties make zeolites interesting for catalysis and separation applications [1-4].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among various potential uses, zeolites have been studied and used as catalysts for NO x removal in exhaust gas treatment systems. Several types of zeolites such as ZSM-5, ferrierite (FER) and mordenite (MOR) modified with transition metal ions have been found to be active catalysts for hydrocarbon and ammonia selective catalytic NO x reduction [5-7]. Also, the zeolite structure with pores of molecular dimension offers a unique shape selectivity effect. A physical mixture of zeolites of different pore systems has been studied as a new generation of catalysts for NO x selective catalytic reductioninaso-calleddual-porezeolitesystem[8-10].Mass

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A thin ZSM-5 film was grown on an 𝛂 -alumina support, resulting in a composite membrane. The membranes were characterized by SEM and adsorption branch n-hexane/helium permporometry. In addition, the permeation of gas mixtures containing NO2, NO, N2 and argon was evaluated. The effect of temperature and gas mixture composition on the component permeation and selectivity was investigated. It was found that NO x permeation through the ZSM-5 membrane was partially surface concentration dependent and was thermally activated. However, transport by gas translational diffusion seemed to dominate at the conditions studied. The presence of various NO x adsorbed species appeared to influence diffusion of NO2 in ZSM-5 and reduced transport of other inert and weakly adsorbed components over a wide temperature range (20-400 ◦ C). Strongly adsorbed surface nitrate species formed in the presence of gas phase NO x should be responsible for the reduced transport of these components at the elevated temperature. The findings are of interest for possible applications of ZSM-5 membranes for component separation at high temperature.

texts.orig: In general, since the pore size in zeolites is in the molecular dimension range, transport of components through the zeolite framework is determined by both adsorption and mobility of the diffusing molecules [11]. The transport is frequently called configurational or intra-crystalline diffusion and the transport rates strongly depend on temperature. Zeolites can also be synthesized in the form of continuous thin films on support materials. When the support material is also porous, the zeolite film and porous support form a composite membrane. However, large pores and defects might also be present in zeolite films through which bulk/molecular diffusion and Knudsen diffusion can contribute to the transport.
texts.text: In general, since the pore size in zeolites is in the molecular dimension range, transport of components through the zeolite framework is determined by both adsorption and mobility of the diffusing molecules [11]. The transport is frequently called configurational or intra-crystalline diffusion and the transport rates strongly depend on temperature. Zeolites can also be synthesized in the form of continuous thin films on support materials. When the support material is also porous, the zeolite film and porous support form a composite membrane. However, large pores and defects might also be present in zeolite films through which bulk/molecular diffusion and Knudsen diffusion can contribute to the transport.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theadsorption properties and mobility of diffusing components can determine the separation selectivity of zeolite membranes. The strongly adsorbing molecules hinder the diffusion of other weakly adsorbing molecules in the membrane. However, at elevated temperature adsorption becomes weaker and mobility of the diffusing components will determine the separation selectivity. Therefore, lowtemperature is required to achieve highly selective separations with zeolite membranes based on differing adsorption properties of components. This understanding of the influence of adsorption of species transported by surface diffusion on selective permeation principally applies to physisorbed species. However, little is known

texts.orig: It was indicated from our previous work [12] that different surface species contribute to NO x adsorption and transport in ZSM-5 films supported on cordierite monoliths. The amount and type of surface species present in the films was temperature dependent and surface nitrates were found to be stable at temperatures as high as 550 ◦ C. Models of the NO x adsorption were also developed to simulate component concentrations in the monolith channels and in the film. The simulations indicated that the adsorption process was determined by component diffusion in the ZSM-5 film and the diffusion appeared to be concentration dependent [13].
texts.text: It was indicated from our previous work [12] that different surface species contribute to NO x adsorption and transport in ZSM-5 films supported on cordierite monoliths. The amount and type of surface species present in the films was temperature dependent and surface nitrates were found to be stable at temperatures as high as 550 ◦ C. Models of the NO x adsorption were also developed to simulate component concentrations in the monolith channels and in the film. The simulations indicated that the adsorption process was determined by component diffusion in the ZSM-5 film and the diffusion appeared to be concentration dependent [13].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In connection with our previous NO x adsorption studies, in the present work the NO x transport through a ZSM-5 membrane was investigated over a wide temperature range. Based on our previous studies [12,13], the presence of surface nitrates at high temperature is expected to play a role in the component permeation at elevated temperatures. A unique contribution of the present work is our aim to understand the influence of high temperature stable adsorbed species on component permeation. This can be of interest for potential applications of ZSM-5 membranes in high temperature separations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ZSM-5 membranes consisting of 500nm thick zeolite films supported on graded porous 𝛂 -alumina discs with a diameter of 25mm were prepared. The supports consisted of a 30 𝛍 m thick top layer with a pore size of 100nm and a 3mm thick bottom layer with a pore size of 3 𝛍 m. The supports were masked and seeded as described previously [14]. The seeded supports were then immersed in a synthesis solution with the composition 3TPAOH:0.25Al2O3:Na2O:25SiO2:1600H2O:100EtOH and the Si/Al ratio of the synthesis solution was thus 50. After the hydrothermal synthesis, the membranes were rinsed and calcined at 500 ◦ C with a heating rate of 0.2 ◦ C/min and a cooling rate of 0.3 ◦ C/min.

texts.orig: Permporometry was carried out in a Wicke-Kallenbach (WK) cell whichhadtwocompartmentsseparatingthetestedmembrane. Each compartment had a diameter of 19mm and was sealed with graphite gaskets to allow gas to diffuse through the membrane. The membranes were dried at 300 ◦ C overnight in a flow of dry helium and then cooled to room temperature. The pressure difference over the membrane was maintained at 1bar and the pressure at the permeate side was atmospheric. At the first measurement point, the feed contained pure helium and the relative pressure of n-hexane was then successively increased to approximately 0.01, 0.025, 0.2, 0.7 and 1.0. At each relative pressure, the system was allowed to equilibrate before the helium permeance was measured with a flowmeter connected after a condenser which removed the n-hexane.Tomaintainthedesiredn-hexanepartialpressure,apure stream of helium was mixed with a flow of helium saturated with n-hexane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AschematicdiagramoftheequipmentusedforNO x permeation measurements is shown in Fig. 1. The prepared ZSM-5 membrane wasmountedinaWicke-Kallenbach(WK)cellwithadesignidentical to that used for the permporometry measurements. The WK cell hadtwocompartmentsandtheinletandoutletpipesforbothcompartments were symmetrically positioned 6mm from the center of the cell. Graphite gaskets (inner diameter 19 mm) were used to seal the cell and allowed the gas to diffuse through the center of the membrane. The cell was placed in a temperature-programmable furnace (F) equipped with a control thermocouple (TC) at the retentate side. Preceding permeation measurements, the membrane was dried at 300 ◦ C for 6 h with a temperature increase of 1 ◦ C/min in an argon flow of 30ml/min in each compartment. Subsequently, the sample was heated at 0.2 ◦ C/min to 400 ◦ C and the temperature was kept constant for 30 min during which the atmosphere was changed to 8% O2 in argon. The pretreatment was continued by re-exposure of the sample to an argon flow and cooling with a temperature decrease of 0.3 ◦ C/min to 300 ◦ C and at 1 ◦ C/min to roomtemperature.Thepurposeofthepretreatmentwastoremove contaminants from the sample either by thermal desorption or by oxidation reactions.

texts.orig: Permeation measurements were carried out by flowing N2, NO, NO2, NO/NO2 or NO2/N2 with a certain concentration in argon from different gas mass flow controllers to the retentate compartment of the WK cell. Meanwhile, a continuous argon flow was fed to the permeate side. The absolute pressure in the retentate and permeate compartments were maintained constant at the same level. To assure the same total pressure was maintained in both compartments, the feed and sweep gases had the same total flow rate of 1000ml/min (STP) and the pressure controllers (PI1 and PI2) were positioned at the same distance from the permeation cell. The permeation measurements were carried out over a temperature range of 20-400 ◦ C. The temperature was increased slowly in a similar way to that used for the sample pretreatment. The permeate side was connected to a chemiluminescence NO x detector (Chemiluminescence CLD 700 EL ht) and mass spectrometer (MS) to measure concentrations of gasses at the permeate outlet. texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 4, the permeance of NO and NO2 increased with temperature over the whole temperature range of 20-400 ◦ C. In the present work, the temperature was increased very slowly so that steady state equilibrium was established throughout the measurements. As shown in the figure, the NO permeance increased almost linearly with temperature. Although the NO2 permeance also increased with temperature, a strict linear increase with temperature was not observed in this case. The increase in permeance suggests that the transport flux of NO and NO2 in the ZSM-5 membrane is strongly activated by temperature. The activated transport observed indicates a domination of configurational type diffusion of components through the membrane [19]. This type of diffusion is dependent on the properties of the diffusing molecule and zeolite such as the ratio of the molecular diameter to channel diameter, zeolite structure and temperature. As indicated by permporometry, the membrane is almost free from defects so that the influence of molecular diffusion through the meso-pore defects should be negligible. The permeance increase with temperature indicates that Knudsen diffusion through the defects should also be insignificant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It was reported that NO is much less adsorbed on ZSM-5 in comparison with NO2 even at low temperature [20,21]. Therefore, in the ZSM-5 micropores, the NO molecules probably remain in a gaseous-like state and the transport would be more likely by gas translational diffusion over the entire temperature range. This type of diffusion is a kind of thermally activated Knudsen diffusion where the pore size of the zeolite is in the range of the diffusing molecule dimension [22]. As seen in Fig. 4, NO2 permeance also increases with temperature over the whole temperature range. This indicates that the NO2 diffusion through the ZSM-5 membrane apparently has the same transport mechanism as that for NO molecules. At low temperatures there is likely some surface diffusion of NO2, however this makes a minor contribution to the permeance since only a small reduction in the increase in NO2 permeance with temperature is observed above about 150 ◦ C as the concentrations of weakly adsorbed surface diffusing species diminishes. In systems or conditions in which surface diffusion is the dominating transport mechanism with MFI membranes the observed permeance profile, like that in Fig. 4, is much different. At low temperatures, first the permeance or transmembrane flux increases to reach a maximum at a temperature less than about 250 ◦ C and then it steadily decreases up until about 350 ◦ C [23].
texts.text: It was reported that NO is much less adsorbed on ZSM-5 in comparison with NO2 even at low temperature [20,21]. Therefore, in the ZSM-5 micropores, the NO molecules probably remain in a gaseous-like state and the transport would be more likely by gas translational diffusion over the entire temperature range. This type of diffusion is a kind of thermally activated Knudsen diffusion where the pore size of the zeolite is in the range of the diffusing molecule dimension [22]. As seen in Fig. 4, NO2 permeance also increases with temperature over the whole temperature range. This indicates that the NO2 diffusion through the ZSM-5 membrane apparently has the same transport mechanism as that for NO molecules. At low temperatures there is likely some surface diffusion of NO2, however this makes a minor contribution to the permeance since only a small reduction in the increase in NO2 permeance with temperature is observed above about 150 ◦ C as the concentrations of weakly adsorbed surface diffusing species diminishes. In systems or conditions in which surface diffusion is the dominating transport mechanism with MFI membranes the observed permeance profile, like that in Fig. 4, is much different. At low temperatures, first the permeance or transmembrane flux increases to reach a maximum at a temperature less than about 250 ◦ C and then it steadily decreases up until about 350 ◦ C [23].

texts.orig: Fig. 4 also shows that the increase in NO permeance is similar for both permeations of NO/Ar and NO/NO2/Ar mixtures. The presence of NO2 in an equal molar amount of NO has no significant influence on the NO permeation through the ZSM-5 membrane. However, over the entire temperature range, the NO2 permeance is apparently higher if NO is present. This indicates that the presence of NO can enhance NO2 transport in the ZSM-5 membrane. Although both components appear to be primarily transported by gas translational diffusion, the permeation of both components through the membrane is inter-dependent which is not consistent with the gas translational diffusion properties.

texts.orig: It was reported that NO2 can be adsorbed on ZSM-5 films and the adsorption results from the formation of various adsorbed species with different adsorption strengths with some species being stable at temperatures greater than 400 ◦ C [12]. The adsorption will of course influence the transport of NO x in the ZSM-5 films. As illustrated by Fig. 4, at low temperature, a steep increase in NO2 permeance is observed when the temperature is increased from 20 to about 50 ◦ C. Due to the adsorption strength and number of the adsorbed molecules, the mobility of the NO2 molecules in the channels at low temperature might be limited. This causes a lower permeation flux of NO2 through the membrane. However, a small temperatureincreaseis able to activate the surface species to partly desorbfromthesurfaceandtomovemoreeasilyintheZSM-5channels. This temperature effect can cause the permeation flux of NO2 to increase significantly. Similar trends were also observed from other permeation measurements as later shown in Fig. 5.

At these high temperatures, NO2 can partially decompose into NO and O2 and thus the NO produced from the NO2 decomposition contributes to the observed permeances. Since temperature ramping during the measurement was slow, the decomposition reaction may always reach an equilibrium level at different locations in the membrane and the amount of NO2 decomposed would therefore be dependent on the amount of NO present. texts.orig: However, Fig. 6 shows that the introduction of 994ppm of NO2 into the feed gas considerably decreases nitrogen permeation through the ZSM-5 membrane over the entire temperature range. A further increase in the NO2 concentration to 1988ppm leads to an additional decrease in the nitrogen permeance. However, the observed dependence of nitrogen permeance on NO2 concentration is not consistent with gas translational diffusion properties for nitrogen. The observations indicate that the presence of NO2 in the ZSM-5 micropores reduce the diffusion of nitrogen molecules, likely because NO2 adsorbs more strongly on the ZSM-5 surface forming species that are stable up to at least 400 ◦ C. These surface species probably reduce the diffusion space and diminish the mobility of nitrogen molecules in the pores of the ZSM membrane.

texts.orig: From the results shown in Fig. 6, it is evident that the presence of NO2 can play an important role in component transport in ZSM5 even at relatively high temperature by means of hindering the mobility of weakly adsorbing molecules in the ZSM-5 pores. Since the adsorbed NO2 remains at the surface in the form of various adsorbed species even at high temperatures due to chemisorption, the species may influence component transport through the pores over a wide temperature range. At low temperature when the surface concentration of the species is sufficient, surface diffusion may contribute to the NO2 transport. Meanwhile, at high temperature the remaining adsorbed species still play a role with interactions between diffusing molecules and the surface species. In addition, the remaining adsorbed species also offer a hindrance effect on the transport of the non-adsorbing molecules.

texts.orig: As previously mentioned, the ZSM-5 membrane used in the presentworkhasamoderatelyhighSi/Alratiowithcationicsodium as the main counter ions. It means that the zeolite contains only a moderate concentration of cationic sites. With exposure to NO2, thermally stable surface nitrates form on the sodium sites. In addition, other weakly adsorbed species may also form at locations nearby at low temperature. Therefore, although the adsorbed NO2 is able to slow down nitrogen diffusion, the surface concentration might not be sufficient to fully block the nitrogen movement in the pores. In addition, the location of the alumina sites in the ZSM-5 with moderately high Si/Al ratio might not be homogeneous [26].

texts.orig: Comparing results presented in Figs. 4 and 6, it is clear that the presence of NO2 can apparently decrease nitrogen permeance but not the permeance of NO. This suggests a different role of the surface nitrate species present in the ZSM-5 films for each diffusing molecule. In the case of nitrogen, a relatively inert and weak-adsorbing molecule with only minor chemical interaction with the surface nitrates, the surface nitrates offer a hindrance effect that lowers the rate of diffusion. However, in the presence of NO as a diffusing molecule, the surface nitrates would also participate in surface reactions and a certain level of NO concentration would result from an equilibrium condition among NO2, NO and the surface nitrates that varies with temperature. In this case, the interaction of the NO with the surface nitrate species, results in the permeance of NO being unaltered by the concentration of NO2.

texts.orig: Fig. 7 shows the effect of total pressure on the permeation of NO andNO2 through the ZSM-5 membrane. During measurements, the total pressure on both retentate and permeate sides of the membrane were kept at the same level to avoid contributions of viscous flow to component permeation through the membrane. As illustrated by the figure, the total pressure ranging from 1.4 to 1.8 bar has no significant influence on the NO and NO2 permeance selectivity. The trends of permeance increasing with temperature are similar for all measurements independent of total pressure. The increase in the total pressure proportionately enlarges the permeation flux of both NO and NO2 but on the other hand it also increases the partial pressure gradient of diffusing species across the membrane. Because NO2 is adsorbed strongly as nitrates, it may be that nearly all of the sites are occupied already at low pressure, which may explain the insignificant influence of the total pressure on the permeation selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thefindings in this paper indicate that ZSM-5 membranes could be used for selective separation of NO x species from N2. This can be of interest for NO x emission abatement from flue gases or automotive exhaust, however the impact of other components in exhaust gases such as water and CO2 on selective permeation will have to also be investigated. It is known that water participates in surface nitrate species formation (reaction (1) above) and CO2 may participate in surface carbonate species formation. However, the moreimportant generally applicable finding from this paper is that under conditions when gas translational diffusion prevails such as at high temperature, the formation of highly stable adsorbed surface species can be used to reduce the diffusion of other weakly adsorbing species. The selective separation of CO2 from flue gases enhanced by carbonate species formation is another area of potential interest.

texts.orig: Permeationofgasmixturescontainingnitrogenoxides,nitrogen and argon through a composite membrane of ZSM-5 film grown on an 𝛂 -alumina support over a wide temperature range was investigated in the present work. The membrane was almost free from defects and the component transport was mainly due to diffusion through ZSM-5 micropores. Over the whole temperature range, the componenttransport was thermally activated and mostly occurred by gas translational diffusion. However, the NO2 transport was influenced by surface concentration with contributions of surface diffusion at low temperature. Meanwhile, NO adsorbed more weakly on the ZSM-5 and its diffusion through the membrane was relatively independent of surface concentration. However, the NO presencecouldreduceNO2 adsorptionandeventuallyenhanceNO2 transport through the membrane. On the other hand, the surface species formed in the presence of NO2 were found to significantly decrease permeation of non-adsorbing molecules (i.e. nitrogen) in the membrane over the entire temperature range. The presence of surface nitrate species seemed able to hinder diffusion of the inert and non-adsorbing molecules in the ZSM-5 micropores at elevated temperatures. However, sufficient amounts of sites for the surface nitrates in ZSM-5 membrane might be necessary to achieve high separation selectivity.
texts.text: Permeationofgasmixturescontainingnitrogenoxides,nitrogen and argon through a composite membrane of ZSM-5 film grown on an 𝛂 -alumina support over a wide temperature range was investigated in the present work. The membrane was almost free from defects and the component transport was mainly due to diffusion through ZSM-5 micropores. Over the whole temperature range, the componenttransport was thermally activated and mostly occurred by gas translational diffusion. However, the NO2 transport was influenced by surface concentration with contributions of surface diffusion at low temperature. Meanwhile, NO adsorbed more weakly on the ZSM-5 and its diffusion through the membrane was relatively independent of surface concentration. However, the NO presencecouldreduceNO2 adsorptionandeventuallyenhanceNO2 transport through the membrane. On the other hand, the surface species formed in the presence of NO2 were found to significantly decrease permeation of non-adsorbing molecules (i.e. nitrogen) in the membrane over the entire temperature range. The presence of surface nitrate species seemed able to hinder diffusion of the inert and non-adsorbing molecules in the ZSM-5 micropores at elevated temperatures. However, sufficient amounts of sites for the surface nitrates in ZSM-5 membrane might be necessary to achieve high separation selectivity.
